title: Malaria Control: Strategy, Economics, and Modeling
text:
The Global Burden

The Toll: ~249 million cases and 608,000 deaths annually (2022/23 data).
The Target: Reducing incidence by 90% by 2030.
The Metric: \(PfPR_{2-10}\) (Plasmodium falciparum Parasite Rate in children aged 2–10).

This is our “gold standard” for measuring community transmission.


[Image of malaria transmission cycle]

title: Malaria Control: Strategy, Economics, and Modeling
text:
The Intervention “Toolbox”








Category
Tool
Mechanism




Vector Control
LLINs & IRS
Kills/Blocks the mosquito vector


Preventative
Vaccines (R21/RTS,S)
Trains the immune system (pre-erythrocytic)


Case Management
RDTs & ACTs
Diagnoses and clears parasites from humans

title: Malaria Control: Strategy, Economics, and Modeling
text:
Why Use Individual-Based Models (IBMs)?
Standard models use differential equations for “compartments” (SIR models). But malaria is complex: * Heterogeneity: Not everyone gets bitten equally. * Age-dependence: Children suffer more severe disease than immune adults. * History: Previous infections change your current risk.

malariasimulation tracks 10,000+ individual agents, each with their own age, net usage, and parasite load.

title: Malaria Control: Strategy, Economics, and Modeling
text:
The Economic Reality: The “Budget Cap”
In public health, we rarely ask “Does it work?” We ask “Is it the best use of our limited dollars?”
Your Challenge: * Total Population: 10,000 people. * Annual Budget: $50,000. * The Goal: Achieve the lowest \(PfPR_{2-10}\) possible without going bankrupt.

title: Malaria Control: Strategy, Economics, and Modeling
section: Group A: The Biological Arms Race
text:
Group A: The Biological Arms Race
Vaccines vs. Drug Resistance
The Dilemma: High vaccine coverage is great but expensive ($25/child). If you spend it all on vaccines, you might use “cheap” drugs that are failing due to resistance.

Task: Compare 80% Vaccine Coverage + Cheap (failing) Drugs vs. 20% Vaccine Coverage + Expensive (effective) Drugs.
Critical Question: Does the vaccine provide enough “herd protection” to ignore drug resistance?

title: Malaria Control: Strategy, Economics, and Modeling
text:
Group B: The Vector Defense
Bed Nets (LLINs) vs. Indoor Spraying (IRS)
The Dilemma: * Nets: Cheap ($4.50), last 3 years, but rely on human behavior. * IRS: Expensive ($7.00/person), lasts 6-12 months, but kills mosquitoes regardless of bed use.

Task: Run a “Universal Net” strategy vs. a “Targeted IRS” strategy.
Critical Question: What happens to the infection rate in Year 2 when the spray wears off?

title: Malaria Control: Strategy, Economics, and Modeling
text:
Group C: “The Last Mile”
Case Management & CHWs
The Dilemma: Prevention is key, but people are already sick. * Passive: Wait for patients to walk 10km to a clinic (40% coverage). * Active: Hire Community Health Workers (CHWs) to find cases ($1,000/worker).

Task: Invest in 20 CHWs vs. investing that $20,000 into more bed nets.
Critical Question: Is it better to stop the next infection or clear the current one?

title: Malaria Control: Strategy, Economics, and Modeling
text:
Instructions for the Lab

Open the Malaria Control Web Tool.
Identify your Group’s assigned intervention sliders.
Phase 1: Try to get \(PfPR\) below 5% with unlimited budget.
Phase 2: Apply the $50,000 constraint.
Phase 3: Present your “Optimal Mix” to the class.


Remember: If your budget bar turns red, your strategy is disqualified!

title: Malaria Control Strategy Lab
text:

title: Malaria Control Strategy Lab
text:
This lab allows you to explore different malaria control strategies given a fixed budget. Adjust the sliders to see the impact on malaria prevalence in children aged 2-10 (\(PfPR_{2-10}\)) and the associated costs.

Lab Instructions

Phase 1: Try to get \(PfPR\) below 5% with unlimited budget.
Phase 2: Apply the $25,000 constraint.
Phase 3: Present your “Optimal Mix” to the class.


Note: If your budget bar turns red, your strategy is disqualified!
